verses.text: In those days John the Baptist appeared, proclaiming in the wilderness of Judea, saying,
“Repent, for the kingdom of heaven has come near.”
For this is the one spoken of by the prophet Isaiah, saying, “A voice of one crying out in the wilderness, ‘Prepare the way of the Lord, make His paths straight.’”
Now John himself had camel-hair clothing, an animal skin belt around his waist, and his food was locusts and wild honey.
Jerusalem, all Judea, and the whole Jordan region went out to him,
and they were being baptized by him in the Jordan River, confessing openly their sins.
But when he saw many Pharisees and Sadducees coming for his baptism, he said, “You brood of vipers! Who warned you to flee from the coming wrath?”
Bear fruit, therefore, worthy of repentance.
Do not suppose in your thinking, “We have Abraham as our father,” for I tell you that God is able to bring to life children for Abraham out of these stones.
The axe is laid at the root of the trees already; and every tree that does not bear good fruit will be cut down and thrown into the fire.
I baptize you with water for repentance, but the one who comes after me is mightier than I, whose sandals I am not worthy to carry. He will baptize you with the Holy Spirit and fire.
His winnowing shovel is in his hand. He will purge his threshing floor, gather his wheat into the storehouse, and burn the chaff with unquenchable fire.
Then Jesus came from Galilee to the Jordan to be baptized by John.
But John tried to prevent him, saying, “I need to be baptized by you, yet you come to me?”
Jesus answered, “Allow it for now, for it is fitting for us to fulfill all righteousness.” Then John allowed him.
As soon as Jesus rose from the waters of baptism, the heavens opened, and he saw the Spirit of God descending like a dove coming to rest upon him.
Behold, a voice came from heaven saying, “This is my beloved Son, in whom I am well pleased.”

footnotes.content: The phrase 'John the Baptist appeared, proclaiming...' establishes his role as the prophetic herald (cf. Malachi 3:1).
The quote from Isaiah 40:3 (LXX) emphasizes the fulfillment of messianic prophecy, with 'way of the Lord' highlighting divine sovereignty.
The phrase 'Jordan region' reflects the geographical focus of the narrative, emphasizing the scope of John’s impact.
The metaphor 'brood of vipers' genne echidnōn underscores the hypocrisy of the Pharisees and Sadducees (cf. Matthew 12:34).
John challenges reliance on physical lineage (Abraham) over true spiritual transformation (cf. Luke 3:8).
The 'Holy Spirit and fire' likely symbolize Jesus' dual role as purifier and judge (cf. Malachi 3:2–3).
The voice from heaven affirms Jesus’ identity as the Son of God, aligning with Psalm 2:7 and the Trinitarian structure of baptism (cf. Matthew 28:19).
